items.title: Never fake parity, production-readiness or 100%
items.description: Status claims must be true. Do not report feature parity, production-readiness, or a 100% figure that the evidence does not support. An honest lower number with named gaps is always preferred to an inflated one that collapses on inspection.
items.applicability: Every status report, README badge and completion claim.
items.example: Rather than 'ERP at 100% parity', the report states '~45% parity, with HR/payroll/POS/e-commerce as skeletons' and lists what remains.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive claims from measured artefacts (tests, gates), not aspiration. Test implication: a status claim is backed by a reproducible check. Generator implication: generated status documents quote measured numbers. Boundary: honesty about scope does not by itself raise the score; it only makes the score trustworthy.

items.title: Hold an explicit classification ladder and state exact remaining blockers
items.description: Maturity is expressed on an explicit ladder — for example PILOT, then LOCAL_PRODUCTION_READY_CORE, and upward — and the current rung is justified by naming the exact blockers preventing the next rung. The ladder replaces a vague 'almost done' with a defensible position.
items.applicability: Communicating where the product stands and what would move it up.
items.example: The product sits at LOCAL_PRODUCTION_READY_CORE with the next rung blocked on external TLS and a signed acceptance, both listed by name.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: define the rungs once and map evidence to each. Test implication: the claimed rung's criteria are all demonstrably met. Generator implication: status generation reads the ladder definition. Boundary: the ladder is a self-assessment framework, not an external certification scheme.

items.title: Separate internal completeness from GA-when-external-proofs-supplied
items.description: Track two distinct things: what the team can complete and prove locally (internal completeness) and what only becomes general-availability once external proofs are supplied. Conflating them lets external dependencies masquerade as done.
items.applicability: Deciding what 'done' means for a release.
items.example: The core is internally complete and tested, but GA is explicitly conditioned on an external security review that the team cannot perform itself.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: maintain two columns — internal done and external-pending — in the status document. Test implication: internal-done items each have a passing local check. Generator implication: status documents render both columns. Boundary: internal completeness is fully provable locally; the GA column depends on parties outside the repository.

items.title: Name external gates that cannot be closed locally
items.description: Gates such as a CA-issued TLS certificate, SSO/OIDC integration, an external security review, and a signed customer acceptance cannot be satisfied from inside the repository. They must be named explicitly as open external gates, never quietly marked done or hidden.
items.example: The handover lists CA TLS, SSO/OIDC, external security review and signed acceptance as the four external gates outstanding for GA.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep external gates as named, tracked items distinct from code tasks. Test implication: no external gate is shown as closed by a local check. Generator implication: generated handover docs enumerate external gates. Boundary: the team can prepare for these gates but cannot close them; closure is external by definition.

items.title: Gate every change on the full stacked check set
items.description: A change is not done until it passes the whole stack: build with zero errors, the full test suite, knowledge-pack verification, the production-readiness gate, and the security audit. Passing one and skipping the rest is not 'done'.
items.applicability: Definition of done for any change during productization.
items.example: A controller tweak is held until build, tests, knowledge-pack verify, readiness gate and security audit all pass.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: wire the stack as a single gate runners can invoke. Test implication: the gate fails fast if any layer fails. Generator implication: generated products inherit the same gate stack. Boundary: passing the stack proves the checks held, not that every possible defect is absent.

items.title: Never commit build, binary, database, secret or screenshot artefacts
items.description: bin, obj, database files, node_modules, .tmp paths, zip archives, keys and screenshots must never enter source control. The repository holds source and specs; everything reproducible or sensitive is git-ignored and regenerated.
items.applicability: Every commit and every cleanup pass.
items.example: A cleanup pass removes a stray obj/ tree and a captured screenshot from staging and confirms .gitignore covers them.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep .gitignore authoritative and re-check it during cleanup. Test implication: a tree scan finds no ignored-class artefact tracked. Generator implication: generated output directories are ignored, not committed. Boundary: gitignore prevents accidental commits but cannot retroactively scrub history without a deliberate rewrite.

items.title: Keep generated products source-only
items.description: A generated product is committed as source and specs only — never with its build output, packed binaries or local database. Consumers rebuild it, which also re-proves the generator's reproducibility.
items.applicability: Storing or sharing a generated solution.
items.example: The generated ERP is committed as .cs, templates and spec; its bin/obj and any LocalDB file are excluded.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: emit into an ignored output area and commit only source. Test implication: the committed product builds from source with no prebuilt binaries present. Generator implication: source-only output is the storage contract. Boundary: source-only storage requires consumers to build, trading convenience for reproducibility and a clean repo.

items.title: Do not publish, tag final v1.0, or merge unless explicitly asked
items.description: Publishing a release, applying a final v1.0 tag, or merging to the main branch are owner decisions. The work prepares for them but does not perform them without an explicit instruction, so a sprint never silently ships.
items.applicability: End-of-sprint actions on a release candidate.
items.example: A sprint leaves a verified release candidate on its branch and reports readiness, without tagging v1.0 or merging until told to.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: stop at the prepared state and report; do not run the publish/tag/merge step. Test implication: the branch is verified but no final tag or merge is present. Generator implication: generated release scaffolding is prepared, not published. Boundary: this restraint governs the action of releasing, not the readiness of the candidate.

items.title: Disclose a transitive dev/test-only advisory honestly
items.description: A transitive advisory that affects only a development or test dependency — for example NU1903 on the SQLite test provider — is disclosed honestly as dev/test-only rather than hidden or suppressed silently. The disclosure states the scope and why it does not reach production.
items.applicability: Security audit reporting of dependency advisories.
items.example: The audit notes NU1903 on the SQLite provider used only in tests, marks it dev/test-only, and explains it is not in the production deployment path.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: classify each advisory by where the dependency runs and disclose accordingly. Test implication: the audit output lists the advisory with its dev/test-only scope. Generator implication: generated audit reports carry the same disclosure shape. Boundary: dev/test-only scope reduces but does not eliminate supply-chain risk, so the advisory still warrants tracking.

items.title: Prove the product with a fresh-clone build every sprint
items.description: Each sprint clones the repository fresh and builds and tests from scratch, proving the product does not depend on undisclosed local state. A green fresh-clone run is part of the readiness evidence.
items.example: A clean clone builds 0-error and runs the suite green, confirming nothing on the developer's machine was silently required.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: run the build from an empty checkout, not a warm tree. Test implication: build is 0-error and tests pass from the fresh clone. Generator implication: the same proof covers generated output built from source. Boundary: fresh-clone proof validates buildability and tests, not runtime behaviour against external services.

items.title: Maintain one authoritative CURRENT_STATUS document
items.description: Productize around a single authoritative current-status document that states the classification rung, internal-vs-external split, open external gates and the last verification result. Scattered or stale status notes are consolidated into it so there is one truth to read.
items.example: CURRENT_STATUS names the rung, lists the four external gates, and records the latest fresh-clone and gate-stack results with dates.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: update the one document as the gate of record, not ad-hoc notes. Test implication: the document's claims match the latest gate-stack run. Generator implication: status generation targets this single file. Boundary: a document is only as current as its last update, so it must be refreshed each sprint.

items.title: Label scores as honest self-assessment, not external certification
items.description: Readiness and parity scores are the team's honest self-assessment, computed from local evidence. They are not, and must not be presented as, external certification. The label travels with the number so no reader mistakes a self-score for an accreditation.
items.applicability: Any published readiness or parity figure.
items.example: A 'production-readiness 8/10 (self-assessed)' badge makes clear no external body issued the score.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: attach the self-assessment label wherever a score is shown. Test implication: a published score carries its self-assessment qualifier. Generator implication: generated badges include the qualifier. Boundary: a self-score can be rigorous yet still differ from what an external auditor would conclude.

items.title: Run a cleanup pass that removes stray artefacts before claiming done
items.description: Before declaring a sprint done, run a repository cleanup pass that finds and removes stray build output, temp files, packed archives and accidental binaries, and confirms .gitignore covers their classes. A clean tree is part of readiness, not an afterthought.
items.example: The cleanup pass deletes a leftover .tmp working directory and a stray zip, then verifies the tree is free of ignored-class artefacts.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: script the cleanup scan so it is repeatable. Test implication: post-cleanup the tree scan reports no stray artefacts. Generator implication: generated output areas are cleaned the same way. Boundary: cleanup tidies the working tree; it does not alter committed history.

items.title: Report the exact gap, not a rounded-up impression
items.description: When a metric is short of target, report the precise shortfall and its cause rather than rounding up to a comforting figure. A '~92% with the gap being the non-deterministic modules' statement is more useful and more honest than 'basically 100%'.
items.example: Reproduction is reported as ~92% of tests with the remainder attributed to named LLM modules, not as 'effectively complete'.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compute the gap and attribute it before reporting. Test implication: the reported gap matches the measured diff. Generator implication: generated reports carry the exact figure and cause. Boundary: an exact gap describes the current measurement, not future closure.

items.title: Verify knowledge packs as part of the release gate
items.description: Knowledge-pack integrity is a release-gating concern: every pack must pass offline validation — valid GUIDs, no duplicate or cross-pack UIDs, non-empty limitations and tags, and an itemCount that matches the actual items — before the release is considered ready.
items.applicability: Any release that ships or relies on the curated knowledge packs.
items.example: The release gate runs the verify-all-knowledge-packs script and blocks if any pack reports a UID, count or limitation failure.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: include pack verification in the stacked gate, not as a manual step. Test implication: a pack with a bad count or duplicate UID fails the gate. Generator implication: generated packs must satisfy the same validation. Boundary: structural verification confirms format integrity, not the substantive quality of each item's content.
